sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Suspected abuse or neglect may affect children, older people, dependent adults, and intimate-partner victims. Prehospital priorities are immediate safety, objective clinical assessment, privacy where possible, careful documentation, and appropriate escalation to police, hospital, or safeguarding pathways.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Injury pattern, explanation, or delay in presentation that does not fit the clinical findings
- Fearfulness, withdrawal, controlling companion behaviour, or inconsistent history
- Signs of neglect such as poor hygiene, dehydration, pressure injury, malnutrition, or unsafe living conditions
- Child, older person, or dependent adult disclosing harm, coercion, or unsafe circumstances

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Abuse should be considered as a potential differential when findings are unexplained or concerning
- Separate the patient from the suspected perpetrator or controlling companion where safe and practical
- Do not use a controlling companion as the interpreter or gatekeeper to the patient
- Do not promise secrecy. Explain that safety concerns may need to be shared with appropriate services
- Use the patient's own words where possible and avoid leading questions or amateur investigation
- Strangulation or choking disclosures should be treated as high risk because they are associated with future serious harm

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey and treatment of acute clinical needs first
- Create privacy where safe so the patient can speak without coercion
- Reassure the patient, listen calmly, and document objective observations and direct disclosures carefully
- Escalate immediately to police if there is imminent danger or a life-threatening situation
- Handover safeguarding concerns directly to the receiving clinician or responsible authority

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Assess capacity where consent, disclosure, or refusal decisions are affected by fear, coercion, or cognitive impairment
- Consider need for senior clinical, police, child-protection, or social-work coordination according to local law and policy
- Preserve forensic value where relevant by avoiding unnecessary disturbance of clothing, bodily evidence, or the scene once lifesaving care is addressed
- Use transport destination and pre-notification planning to support the patient's safety needs as well as their medical needs

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- This guideline complements, but does not replace, the more specific domestic and family violence pathway
- Child abuse, elder abuse, neglect, and intimate-partner violence may overlap, so keep the approach broad and safety focused
- Document who was present, what was said, observed injuries or environmental concerns, and who the concern was handed over to
